issues.title: Thomas Fossati ' comments
issues.author: junzhanghw
issues.body:
Achieved from 
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/SUGlc6vgg5CoViVh1dkk8c6PiOU/

1. Many of the challenges described (cross-domain visibility, delegation
chains, evolving authorisation) apply to *any* complex distributed
microservice architecture.  Unsure of what makes this problem strictly
unique to AI agents?  Could this work be generalised to something like
"dynamic workload delegation" without the AI buzzword? :-)

2. There seems to be quite a lot of overlap with WIMSE, OAUTH, SCITT,
RATS -- at least, which is both good and bad.  To minimise the risk of
overlap, conflict, et cetera, I suggest you make the proposal visibile
to these groups (if you haven't already done it).  I understand it may
be a bit too early to properly engage, but a quick heads-up would not
hurt (_maybe_).

3. It is not entirely clear to me how does this work would differ from
existing distributed tracing standards, e.g., OpenTelemetry / W3C Trace
Context?  Is the goal to add cryptographic proof to traces, or to define
an entirely new transport mechanism?

4. The charter proposes linking user-facing interactions (the prompts)
with system-facing interactions (the API calls) across domains.  How is
the architecture going to handle the mega privacy implications of
transmitting user prompts / intent across administrative boundaries?

5. What is the threat model for the audit data itself?  I may be missing
something but I don't get how we can ensure that a compromised agent
cannot forge/alter/drop its own audit trails before they are recorded?

6. The charter mentions that AI agent behavior may be non-deterministic.
If an agent hallucinates a step or takes an odd path, is the intention
to standardise a data model that can also capture "why" an agent did
something, rather than just "what" it did?

7. The chapter mentiones the need to distinguish between user, agent,
and service identities.  I hope this doesn't require new identity
primitives and instead leverages existing ones like SPIFFE IDs, DIDs &
co.  It'd help if this was made more explitic.

8. I am especially ignorant in this space, so it's a bit unclear to me
what the target audience is for this audit data?  Is it for end-users to
see what an agent did on their behalf, for compliance folks to audit an
organisation, or for downstream APIs to decide whether to authorise an
action?  Or all of the the above?  I guess tHe answer to this may have a
major impact on the data model.

8. Have major AI framework developers expressed interest in implementing
any interoperable standards that would come out of this pipeline?

comments.author: junzhanghw
comments.body:
comments from Thomas Howe
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/gfVRfv94g-eTtH9qZOlzxUCEVd0/

(At least) one of your questions lands straight into the VCON working group:

> 
> The charter proposes linking user-facing interactions (the prompts) with
> system-facing interactions (the API calls) across domains. How is the
> architecture going to handle the mega privacy implications of transmitting
> user prompts / intent across administrative boundaries?
> 
> 

VCONs have a really neat facility in that you can take an original, create a data-reduced version of it to share, that points back to the original. ( https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-vcon-vcon-core-02.html#redacted-object ) All of that can be recorded in SCITT.  So,  you can control the information that crosses security boundaries without losing provenance, integrity and data subject consent.

Also, there's an answer for

> 
> "What is the threat model for the audit data itself? I may be missing
> something but I don't get how we can ensure that a compromised agent
> cannot forge/alter/drop its own audit trails before they are recorded?"
> 
> 
> 
> 

You are not missing anything, and a compromised agent absolutely can lie about its own records. The architecture starts from "the Agent is not trusted" (Section 8). Defence against forgery/omission comes from three independent sources:

* the service the agent calls produces its own corroborating record on its side of the wire,

* consequential actions are registered with a transparency service, so omission and equivocation become detectable across relying parties, and

* RATS attestation can bind records to a known execution environment, raising the bar for silent tampering.

None of these layers alone is sufficient; combined, they let a relying party or auditor detect inconsistencies even when the agent's audit store is fully under the attacker's control. A proper threat model is one of the things the WG should produce.

comments.author: junzhanghw
comments.body:
comments from Thomas
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/dyZGwcq6aObZgZhfyoOrUpOWWTM/

it seems that AUDIT and VCON are
complementary, at the very least, and it'd be worth adding VCON to the
set of identified technology overlaps alongside RATS, SCITT & co.
Could AUDIT perhaps reuse the VCON data model for packaging interactions
on the user-to-agent interface, for example?

issues.title: Jun zhang's comment
issues.author: junzhanghw
issues.body:
Achieved from
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/3BigB5-BvkiMHs0_PNbvvVN4c0c/



1. I think scalability is a key concern, specifically regarding the potential for state explosion (keeping track of too many states). This should be carefully considered.

2. Additionally, the definition and detection of LLM hallucinations might be another issue that requires further study.

comments.author: mirjak
comments.body: new issues created on new repo.

issues.title: Michael Richardson‘s comments
issues.author: junzhanghw
issues.body:
Achieved from
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/ZX6mUUD8htUCyOFt7OfnD0-KKPw/


1. Terminology & Naming

- On the term "AI":

"AI" is a decade specific marketing term, and has negative technical content. It used to mean "plays chess", then it meant "Expert System", and then... What we have today are machine learning systems based upon LLM. Please say so once, and then use the term "Agent" in the rest of the charter.

- On the acronym "AUDIT":

I am, however, concerned that the TLA AUDIT, does not on its own convey much without knowing its expansion. Pass that TLA through a few layers of PHB, and too much context is lost, and it can mean all sorts of things way out of our scope.

- Proposal for a new name:

maybe something like: AGENTIC = AGent Engagement with Non-repudable Traceable In Cross-domain

2. Architecture Document Details

- On the diagram label:

("Attestation" alone in a diagram that Henk wrote? Surely, that should be "Attestation Results"?)

- On Section 7 (JSON Example):

I like that section 7 is trying to provide an Illustration of an EXAMPLE, but perhaps the use of what looks like very concrete JSON will confuse some. Is there a way to be slightly less detailed here?

3. Granular Authorization Complexity

- On the need for detailed permissions:

On the whole, I think that Agentic uses are going to force most resource providers to create many low-level detailed permissions that can be seperately delegatable. It's not enough to just split out every day operations from accounting/data affecting ones. Many of the every day operations will need to be split into multiple authorizations.

- On historical failures (VMS/NTFS):

Many systems have a primitive two-level view... There is a long history of the challenges of very detailed permissions problems. VMS and NTFS have quite detailed lists of permissions, and it was often difficult for administrators to know exactly what permissions were needed for a particular operation. After three iterations with the admin trying to figure things out, the admin would often just throw every possible permission at the user in frustration.

- On the risk of "Overly specific" vs "too coarse":

Overly specific and too coarse permissions/authorizations are a problem. We continue to see some of this legacy in some of the local priviledge escalation attacks against core desktop security tools like MS-Defender: it has privileges beyond what one would think it needs because it has to get around somethings else.

4. Standardization of Authorization Description

- Critique of OAuth2 approach:

OAUTH2 has, wisely I think, punted on the problem of what is being authorized to the resource owner. This made sense at the time, although I think it has put too much power into a few resource owners. I do not think that it will be effective for this WG to do the same.

- The "Rabbit Hole" warning:

At the time, we also can't go down the rabbit hole of trying to define an entire ontology of authorizations required for any particular target (i.e., "booking a hotel room" or seting up a pre-authorized debit for "a municipal tax authority")

- The Proposal (Mechanism for Experts):

What I think that we can do is create a concise process for experts in that domain to describe their authorization ontology. Yes, it will need to be machine readable.

- Challenge to the Charter (Scope):

so I don't know how we can audit delegations of authorizations without having a good way to describe them. Particularly across "ADMINISTRATIVE" boundaries.

Requirement for Relationships:

At least, I think that we need to know if a FINE-GRAINED authorization is part of some other coarse-grained authorization, and we might also have situations where there are multiple coarse-grained authorizations which contain the same FINE-GRAINED authorization.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body: These changes to charter seem to have been executed already in: https://github.com/mirjak/audit-bof-preparation/commit/78c571c941642424a64bcbe9144899cf9c0b93b1

comments.author: mirjak
comments.body:
@mcr yes I created a separate issues for your points 1) and 4) in the new repo here:

https://github.com/mirjak/audit-bof-preparation/issues/1
https://github.com/mirjak/audit-bof-preparation/issues/11
https://github.com/mirjak/audit-bof-preparation/issues/16

For the two points in 2) it would be good to create separate small issues in this repo (as they relate to the draft).

However, I not quite sure what to do about 3). Is that related to the charter directly or more general?

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
> However, I not quite sure what to do about 3). Is that related to the charter directly or more general?

Nor am I at this point.
These are comments on how an ontology of authorizations would be constructed.
We (computer-using humans) have gotten this wrong repeatedly.  

I am not yet sure if an ontology of authorizations is in charter or not.
I have suggested that we should not attempt to create any such ontology, but rather we should find a way (a technique, a process, a way to describe/encode) to make these ontology easier.  (Spell checker does not think ontologies is a word...)
Perhaps 

1. there is work that belongs in a recharter.
2. that prior to writing that part, we need some input from people with such expertise to educate us
3. maybe there is external work (of which I'm certainly ignorant) which should just be cited/used ... encoded-in-CDDL

Probably such things as (2) do not need to said now in the charter, and can be included by WG chairs into agenda based upon chair's fiat, until such time as we can charter in the right thing.

comments.author: mirjak
comments.body:
I think we should not explicitly add this to the chapter (now). This might come up during working group discussion but I do hope that this is actually more a question for other groups like oauth and we "just" put in the record whatever they provide.

If you roughly agree, can we maybe just close the issue for now?

issues.title: Yuqiang's comments
issues.author: junzhanghw
issues.body:
Achieved from
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/cZRbivCQvViTXmoO_vtg3ASLCZE/

Before moving toward a BoF, it may be useful to review these related efforts and clarify the boundaries: what belongs to the overall audit architecture, what belongs to identity and authorization, what belongs to delegation provenance, what belongs to judgment/accountability events, what belongs to receipts/provenance, and what should remain reusable across these layers.

for example

draft-sharif-agent-audit-trail



draft-nelson-agent-delegation-receipts



draft-helixar-hdp-agentic-delegation



draft-wang-jep-judgment-event-protocol



draft-klrc-aiagent-auth



draft-munoz-wimse-authorization-evidence



draft-munoz-scitt-permit-profile



draft-somoza-atn-agent-trust-negotiation



other provenance/receipt-oriented drafts

comments.author: mirjak
comments.body: moved to new repo and reply by email. No action here.

issues.title: Chris Hood’s comments
issues.author: junzhanghw
issues.body:
Achieved from
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/R5vtkX7ipQKY8oK8-WKu_uo3Jx8/

Everyone is trying to solve this at the http level, by bolting on and manipulating a protocol that was never designed for agents. Meanwhile, AGTP is solving for all of these issues and more, within a dedicated layer.

I have a charter, vision, BoF proposal as well in the process.

Charter: [https://github.com/nomoticai/agtp-ietf/blob/main/CHARTER.md](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/R5vtkX7ipQKY8oK8-WKu_uo3Jx8/%3Ca%20href=)" target="_blank" rel="nofollow">https://github.com/nomoticai/agtp-ietf/blob/main/CHARTER.md
Vision: [https://github.com/nomoticai/agtp-ietf/blob/main/VISION.md](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/R5vtkX7ipQKY8oK8-WKu_uo3Jx8/%3Ca%20href=)" target="_blank" rel="nofollow">https://github.com/nomoticai/agtp-ietf/blob/main/VISION.md

With conversations happening currently with multiple enterprises regarding implementing AGTP.

comments.author: mirjak
comments.body: This is about a different proposal, so I don't think we can address this here without providing further detailed comment on the auditing part only.

issues.title: Orie's comments
issues.author: junzhanghw
issues.body:
Achieved from
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/pIzlRmmmBTIAOmm9hJxHDxz4hmw/

1) Is there general interest in agent auditing work in the IETF? Are there
other people who maybe already work on this topic or a planning to?

I'm interested in this work, particularly the OpenTelemetry
(OTEL) and trajectory aspects.
I'm suspicious of grand architecture though, it feels like a lot of this
layer is being tackled with Langfuse/LangSmith given the speed of iteration
in open source, is standardization of this layer beneficial or likely to be
adopted?

2) Any comments on the proposed scope of the work? Is the scope clear? Is
the IETF the right place for this work? Does this need a new working group?

It probably needs its own WG... especially if it builds off other working
groups but is just narrowed to agentic domain modeling.

3) Any comments on the proposed architecture or any additional work in the
space!

I worry a lot about the scope of deliverable 2... feels, like there is a
lot of room to invent there, but maybe all that is needed is mapping of
existing success stories?
A single deliverable with a clearer scope could probably make this section
better.

I might suggest deferring 3 to future work, or other groups... seems a
mistake to extend oauth outside the oauth wg.

4). probably too soon for a BCP, I would cut it...

... so in summary, I think 1 and 2 could be useful, especially if 2 is "no
new inventions, just mappings for ietf protocols that are being mapped
differently by everyone running custom otel stuff".

comments.author: mirjak
comments.body: These comments have been added to the issues in the new repo.

issues.title: Yaroslav Rosomakho's comments on the arch draft
issues.author: junzhanghw
issues.body:
Comments related to the draft from
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/sN24EBWv1kD7xTzP6O2LdSCqgbs/

 5.  There seems to be too much emphasis on attestation. Attestation is
   clearly important, but it cannot be required for all useful audit
   deployments.
   
  6. Privacy considerations appear mostly late in the document. The
   architecture itself still assumes propagation of trace IDs, parent IDs,
   delegation chains, and actor identity across boundaries. I would stress the
   importance of opaque references, selective disclosure, or encrypted fields
   especially concerning cross-domain audit.
   
  7. The architecture suggests that full prompts and model responses are
   expected to be recorded. These may contain personal data and other
   sensitive information. The architecture should support recording
   audit-relevant information and approval signals without requiring full
   conversational content.

   8. Is there a good reason to restrict architecture to "AI" agents? I
   think it could work for deterministic software where users can delegate
   some tasks.

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
>     6. Privacy considerations appear mostly late in the document. The
>        architecture itself still assumes propagation of trace IDs, parent IDs,
>        delegation chains, and actor identity across boundaries. I would stress the
>        importance of opaque references, selective disclosure, or encrypted fields
>        especially concerning cross-domain audit.

*selective disclosure* <-- emphasizing.

>     8. Is there a good reason to restrict architecture to "AI" agents? I
>        think it could work for deterministic software where users can delegate
>        some tasks.

I completely agree.

comments.author: mirjak
comments.body:
> > ```
> > 8. Is there a good reason to restrict architecture to "AI" agents? I
> >    think it could work for deterministic software where users can delegate
> >    some tasks.
> > ```
> 
> 
> I completely agree.

I agree. As I explained in my email reply to Thomas it wasn't the intention to restrict this but more have AI agents as Amin use case. I created PR #13 to address this part.

issues.title: LUCIA CABANILLAS RODRIGUEZ‘s comments
issues.author: junzhanghw
issues.body:
achieved from 
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/ucCt6vdc2v_RN53Ylbkq2BiAn4k/


1. On making policy evolution a first-class auditable object:

"One aspect that may deserve additional emphasis is making policy evolution and authorization provenance first-class auditable objects, not only the resulting actions. In many AI-agent scenarios, understanding why a policy changed, which context or behavioural signals triggered it, and which authority or component approved or derived the change may become as important as auditing the final action itself."

2. On standardizing evidence correlation across trust boundaries:

"I think it could be useful to explicitly consider standardized mechanisms for correlating evidence across domains, especially when delegation chains span multiple trust boundaries with partially shared policies and heterogeneous enforcement models."

3. On ensuring the audit structure supports specific relationships:

"I think there is a strong connection with the need you identify for a common and interoperable audit structure capable of correlating identities, delegation chains, authorization transitions, and causal relationships across domains and over time. The ability to consistently represent trace identifiers, preceding-event relationships, 'on-behalf-of' delegation semantics, authorization scope, validity, and policy provenance becomes critical once agents dynamically derive, transform, and share authorization state during execution."

4. On connecting policy provenance mechanisms to the auditing architecture:

"I think those provenance mechanisms could naturally complement the auditing architecture, particularly for reconstructing how a specific authorization state or decision was reached at a given point in time."

5. On integrating with existing policy sharing models:

"The draft focuses on interoperable representation and distribution of authorization policies across heterogeneous and automated environments using a common YANG-based model. While it already considers accounting aspects, I think it would need further evolution to fully support auditing."

comments.author: mcr
comments.body:
> achieved from https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/ucCt6vdc2v_RN53Ylbkq2BiAn4k/
>     2. On standardizing evidence correlation across trust boundaries:
> 
> 
> "I think it could be useful to explicitly consider standardized mechanisms for correlating evidence across domains, especially when delegation chains span multiple trust boundaries with partially shared policies and heterogeneous enforcement models."

There is a specification called https://opentelemetry.io/ which might be useful to doing this correlation.

comments.author: mirjak
comments.body:
I created two new specific issues the policy/provenance aspects and delegation chains, see here: #15  and #16

The part related to the work on YANG model has been added to the issue in the charter repo on "relation to existing working groups"

@mcr note that OpenTelemetry has been mentioned already by Orie and is noted in the other repo in the issue on "Relation to existing logging/monitoring works". However, I would be really interesting to learn more about OpenTelemetry and how it could maybe be extended to also cover audit use cases. I think that would be a good discussion for the mailing list.

In favour of the other new issues I will close this issue now.

issues.title: toshiyuki sato's comments
issues.author: junzhanghw
issues.body:
achieved from 
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/_yXD3mlY7PbexK0H-EoMq6Xo7Gs/

The result is that the audit record contains a pre-action statement of
intent that is structurally prior to the action, making ex-post fabrication
of reasoning impossible in a conforming implementation. "Why" is captured
before "what" happens, not reconstructed after.

This is the architectural distinction Chris Hood flagged in this thread —
pre-commitment intent declaration is different in kind from post-hoc audit
recording. The two compose: IDP provides the "why before what" record;
GAR-00 provides the non-suppressible session audit that carries it.

https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-sato-soos-idp/
https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-sato-soos-gar/

issues.title: Diego R. Lopez's comments
issues.author: junzhanghw
issues.body:
Achieved from
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/7ynLOIMhS9KvmsMOpSkq743YK2w/


1) Is there general interest in agent auditing work in the IETF? Are there other people who maybe already work on this topic or a planning to?

I do believe this is rather interesting work though, as Thomas said, I think it would be much more interesting if we refrain from limiting it to the fashionable agents, and it is made clear this would be applicable to any workload (to use the WIMSE terminology) or, if you want to keep the “A” in the proposed name, to any *actor* able to interact with other actors and services in the Internet. Even a broad enough definition of the term “agent” would do...

I am aware this would imply the need of defining the “actor” term, but I think the extra work would be justified by the evident advantages of generality, and also by not tying the fate of this proposal to something that could follow the not-so-glorious path of other past hot topics.

Regarding the second part of the question, we have been working in mechanisms that could be applicable to this idea, such as the draft on YANG data provenance (suitable to be generalized in this context, see https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-opsawg-yang-provenance/) another draft on identity chaining (where the “actor” term is used intentionally, see https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-mw-oauth-actor-chain/) and an open-source approach to evidence collection we had the intention to bring to the IETF soon.

2) Any comments on the proposed scope of the work? Is the scope clear? Is the IETF the right place for this work? Does this need a new working group?

The scope seems clear to me, though I would prefer to make it less “agentical”. In despite of having many potential intersections with other WGs, or even precisely because of this, I believe the proposed work deserves a specific WG.

Regarding the deliverables, I think it could be interesting to make deliverable (1) identify those protocols and models suitable to be considered in the deliverables mentioned in (3), to avoid any future profile creep when trying to address those documents.

And I agree with Lionel that the mention in (1) to only user-facing and system-facing signals seems a little bit limiting, excluding agent/actor chains.

3) Any comments on the proposed architecture or any additional work in the space!

Let me insist in two of my previous comments:


  1.
Make it less “agentical”, with either agents as a relevant (may the most relevant) use case, or a definition of “agent” that is wide enough to accommodate other use cases.
  2.
Consider in the introduction (and the diagram in Figure 1) the idea of a chain of agents/actors

comments.author: mirjak
comments.body: All comments have been moved to the new charter repo or into (one) new issue.

issues.title: Consider in the introduction (and the diagram in Figure 1) the idea of a chain of agents/actors
issues.author: mirjak
issues.body: I created this issue based on feedback from Diego Lopez, see here: https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/7ynLOIMhS9KvmsMOpSkq743YK2w/

issues.title: Make policy evolution and authorization provenance first-class auditable objects
issues.author: mirjak
issues.body:
Feedback from Lucia Cabanillas Rodriguez:
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/ucCt6vdc2v_RN53Ylbkq2BiAn4k/

Regarding the proposed architecture itself, I think the overall direction makes a lot of sense. One aspect that may deserve additional emphasis is making policy evolution and authorization provenance first-class auditable objects, not only the resulting actions. In many AI-agent scenarios, understanding why a policy changed, which context or behavioural signals triggered it, and which authority or component approved or derived the change may become as important as auditing the final action itself.

issues.title: Consider cross-domain delegation chains
issues.author: mirjak
issues.body:
Feedback from Lucia Cabanillas Rodriguez:
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/ucCt6vdc2v_RN53Ylbkq2BiAn4k/

Similarly, I think it could be useful to explicitly consider standardized mechanisms for correlating evidence across domains, especially when delegation chains span multiple trust boundaries with partially shared policies and heterogeneous enforcement models.

issues.title: Highlight multiple (auditor) domains in the diagram
issues.author: mirjak
issues.body:
Comment by Jari Arkko:
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/9zGt68jc6YLEsl1Ipps4B4gDdHo/

In the architecture draft Figure 1 presents an arrangement where there is one auditing service. I wonder if that is the case in reality, or even if your drawing reflects what the rest of the document says, as it does say that the entities produce records independently. And as you say, there are multiple administrative domains involved. Perhaps a drawing how such domains interact would be useful. Or maybe that’s work that we still need to do.

pulls.title: Explicit note to compose existing protocols
pulls.author: mirjak
pulls.body: This addition was proposed by Thomas Howe.

pulls.title: remove AI from Agents as central entity
pulls.author: mirjak
